caption: Fig. 1. Overview of the ROMAN transformation. Left: schematic multiscale construc- tion. Each scale s is partitioned into windows of common length Lbase, and the window index w records coarse temporal position within that scale. Right: Example of the stacked pseudochannels generated by applying ROMAN (S=2) to a real time series.
description2: The figure plots a comparison between a schematic multiscale construction on the left and an example of stacked pseudochannels on the right, showing scales partitioned into windows.
description3: Two-panel schematic illustrates a multiscale construction on the left, and stacked pseudochannels generated from a time series on the right.
questions: What is the overall purpose of the multiscale construction shown?
How are the data organized after the ROMAN transformation is applied?

caption: Fig. 2. Synthetic mechanism studies for (a) coarse position awareness and (b) long- range correlation. Left: representative examples. Right: mean test accuracy over ten realizations, comparing the baseline representation with ROMAN at S = 4.
description2: A line chart plots the mean test accuracy of synthetic mechanism studies, comparing a baseline to ROMAN at S = 4, showing representative examples and mean values.
description3: Two-panel plot displays representative examples and mean test accuracy data, comparing baseline and ROMAN models for coarse position awareness and long-range correlation.
questions: How do different models perform in representing coarse position awareness and long-range correlation?
What is the general trend in test accuracy when comparing baseline and ROMAN representations?

caption: Fig. 3. Synthetic mechanism studies for (c) multiscale interaction and (d) full posi- tional invariance. Left: representative examples. Right: mean test accuracy over ten realizations, comparing the baseline representation with ROMAN at S = 4.
description2: A line chart plots the mean test accuracy of a synthetic mechanism study for multiscale interaction and full positional invariance, comparing a baseline representation to ROMAN at S=4.
description3: Two-panel plot displays time series data in the left panel and a table comparing baseline and ROMAN model test accuracy in the right panel.
questions: How do the examples illustrate a comparison of multiscale interaction or positional invariance?
What is the general trend in test accuracy when comparing different model representations?

caption: Fig. 4. Baseline (S = 1) vs. ROMAN-transformed data (S ∈ {2, 3, 4}) results for the MiniRocket and FCN Classifier models. We present the per-dataset UCR accuracies and the scaling of computational times with respect to S.
description2: The figure plots baseline versus Roman-transformed data results for MiniRocket and FCN Classifier models, showing per-dataset UCR accuracies and computational time scalings.
description3: Three-panel plot displays scatter plots comparing baseline and ROMAN-transformed data accuracies, alongside line charts showing scaling of training and inference times.
questions: How do the accuracies of the models change with different data transformations?
What is the relationship between data scaling and computational time?

caption: Fig. 5. Critical-difference diagrams for the five-model hard-voting ensemble study on UCR and UEA. Each diagram compares the eight ensemble variants within the corre- sponding archive: the four baseline-only ensembles and their four mixed-scale ROMAN counterparts. Lower average rank is better.
description2: The figure presents critical-difference diagrams comparing eight ensemble variants, including four baseline-only and four mixed-scale ROMAN models, across two archives (UCR and UEA).
description3: Four-panel diagrams, arranged in a 2x2 grid, compare eight ensemble variants across UCR and UEA datasets, using lines to show relative ranking.
questions: How do different ensemble variants perform relative to each other in these datasets?
What is the general organization of the results when comparing ensemble performance?
